full_text:
1 
2 
3 
4 
5 
6 
7 
8 UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT 
9 SOUTHERN DISTRICT OF CALIFORNIA 
10 
11 BRIAN LUNDSTROM, Case No.: 18-cv-2856-GPC-MSB 
12 Plaintiff, 
 ORDER GRANTING DEFENDANTS' 
13 v. MOTION FOR SUMMARY 
 JUDGMENT 
14 CARLA YOUNG, an individual; 
 LIGAND PHARMACEUTICALS, INC.; 
15 [ECF No. 215, 216–217, 219, 229, 232, 
 LIGAND PHARMACEUTICALs, INC. 
 242] 
16 401(k) PLAN; KOONSFULLER, PC and 
 DOES 1 through 20, 
17 
 Defendants. 
18 
19 
 Pending before the Court is a Motion for Summary Judgment filed by Defendants 
20 
 Ligand Pharmaceuticals, Inc. ("Ligand") and Ligand Pharmaceuticals, Inc. 401(K) Plan 
21 
 ("the Plan").1 ECF No. 216. A hearing was held on March 29, 2024. For the reasons 
22 
 stated below, the Motion is GRANTED. 
23 
24 
25 
 1 Also pending is Defendants' Motion to Exclude Expert Testimony. ECF No. 215. That 
26 motion is DENIED as moot. The Parties' Motions to Seal and Defendants' Request for 
 Judicial Notice are GRANTED. ECF Nos. 217, 219, 229, 232. 
27 
1 BACKGROUND 
2 At the heart of this six-year-old case rests a Qualified Domestic Relations Order 
3 ("QDRO"), signed by a state court judge in Texas and served upon Ligand in December 
4 of 2017, directing the transfer of Plaintiff's entire 401(k) savings to his ex-wife, Carla 
5 Young. ECF No. 120. Ligand notified Plaintiff of the QDRO in January of 2018. Id. at 
6 3. Plaintiff immediately sought to invalidate the QDRO in the Texas state appellate 
7 system, but those challenges failed. Id. at 4, 6. On February 13, 2018, following the 
8 Texas Court of Appeals' denial of Plaintiff's Writ of Mandamus, Ligand initiated the 
9 distribution of Plaintiff's entire 401(k) account balance to his ex-wife. ECF No. 216-2 at 
10 ¶ 27. Plaintiff then brought this federal suit on December 20, 2018, raising twelve claims 
11 for relief, including renewed attempts to invalidate the QDRO. He filed his First 
12 Amended Complaint ("FAC") on June 4, 2019, and his Second Amended Complaint on 
13 May 25, 2022. This Court determined, in its Order on Defendants' Motions to Dismiss, 
14 that Plaintiff was collaterally estopped by the state court judgment from challenging the 
15 validity of the QDRO, and the Court dismissed each of Plaintiff's claims that attempted 
16 to relitigate the issue in federal court. ECF No. 120. Only three claims remain: 
17 Plaintiff's first cause of action, which alleges that Ligand violated ERISA by distributing 
18 his 401(k) funds prematurely; Plaintiff's fourth cause of action, which alleges that Ligand 
19 violated ERISA by failing to provide him with its written procedures for determining the 
20 qualified status of the domestic relations order; and Plaintiff's twelfth cause of action, 
21 which alleges that Ligand retaliated against him for filing the instant lawsuit. Id. Further 
22 factual development is reserved for the discussion below.2 
23 
24 
25 
 2 Plaintiff filed a motion to strike Defendant's Responses to Plaintiff's Separate 
26 Statement of Undisputed Facts. ECF No. 242. The Court does not rely upon those 
 response and thus DENIES the motion as moot. 
27 
1 STANDARD 
2 Summary judgment is appropriate where "the pleadings, depositions, answers to 
3 interrogatories, and admissions on file, together with the affidavits, if any, show that 
4 there is no genuine issue as to any material fact and that the moving party is entitled to a 
5 judgment as a matter of law." Fed. R. Civ. P. 56(c). The movant bears the initial burden 
6 to identify the portions of the record that demonstrate an absence of a genuine issue of 
7 material fact. See Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317, 322 (1986). When the non-
8 moving party bears the burden of proof, the movant need only demonstrate that there is 
9 an absence of evidence to support the claims of the non-moving party. Celotex, 477 U.S. 
10 at 322. If the moving party meets this initial burden, the non-moving party must set forth 
11 "specific facts showing that there is a genuine issue for trial." Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, 
12 Inc., 477 U.S. 242, 250 (1986). 
13 DISCUSSION 
14 I. Premature Distribution 
15 Plaintiff's first remaining cause of action (his first cause of action) alleges that the 
16 distribution of his 401(k) savings, when he was but fifty-five-years old, was premature. 
17 He claims that, under ERISA and the terms of the 401(k) plan, distribution was not 
18 permissible until he turned fifty-nine and a half. ECF No. 228 at 16. 
19 But Plaintiff fails to adequately identify how premature distribution harms him.3 
20 See Gatti v. Reliance Standard Life Ins. Co, 415 F.3d 978, 984 (9th Cir. 2005) (quoting 
21 Jebian v. Hewlett Packard Co., 310 F.3d 1173 (9th Cir. 2002)) (cleaned up) ("Jebian 
22 clarified that a claimant will only be entitled to substantive remedies for procedural 
23 violations of ERISA if the claimant can establish that the violation resulted in substantive 
24 harm."). Plaintiff explains that "[a]s a direct and proximate result of Ligand's violation 
25 

26 
 3 The Court ordered supplemental briefing on the issue at the March 29, 2024, hearing. 
27 
1 of the 401(k) Plan's terms and their breach of fiduciary duty, Plaintiff has been harmed 
2 because the 401(k) investment assets are no longer in his account and instead are 
3 wrongfully in the possession of Ms. Young." ECF No. 92 at 14. Even if the investment 
4 assets remained in Plaintiff's account, however, he could not access or derive any benefit 
5 from them. The QDRO provides in relevant part: 
6 This Order awards, assigns and grants to Alternate Payee an amount equal to 
 one-hundred percent (100%) of Participant's total account balance, whether 
7 
 vested or unvested, in the Plan or accumulated under the Plan as of the date 
8 the Court signs this order, plus any interest, earnings, investment income, 
 gains, and increase, or losses, attributable thereon from that date until the date 
9 
 of total distribution to Alternate Payee. 
10 ECF No. 216-5 at 103. Under the QDRO, the 401(k) assets no longer belonged to 
11 Plaintiff—they had been assigned entirely to Ms. Young—and thus the Court fails to see 
12 how Plaintiff may have been harmed. Cf. Borda v. Hardy, Lewis, Pollard Page, 138 F.3d 
13 1062, 1069 (6th Cir. 1998) ("Because Mr. Borda could not expect to resume his 
14 employment with the law firm after the firm's dissolution, it could make no difference to 
15 him whether the funds in the suspense account were distributed to the other participants 
16 early in 1995 or a year-and-a-half later. Mr. Borda could not get the funds either way . . . 
17 . It had no adverse effect at all on Mr. Borda's interests, and Mr. Borda was in no 
18 position to complain of a premature distribution of funds that were clearly never going to 
19 be his."). 
20 In supplemental briefing on the matter, Plaintiff argues that "[h]ad those funds 
21 remained segregated in Ligand's Plan, the Court could have remedied Plaintiff by 
22 providing equitable relief . . . ." ECF No. 244 at 2. He explains that the "harm is based 
23 on Plaintiff's inability to go after those funds that have been transferred." Id. He 
24 provides no citation for either of these contentions. Plaintiff's argument wholly fails to 
25 acknowledge the fact that regardless of the timing of the distribution, Plaintiff had no 
26 
27 
1 right to the underlying funds under the QDRO. He ignores this point entirely, and 
2 accordingly, the Court finds his argument unpersuasive. 
3 Even assuming that Plaintiff had demonstrated harm, Defendants correctly argue 
4 that Plaintiff's claim would fail on the merits because immediate distribution was 
5 consistent with the Plan terms and ERISA. The QDRO required distribution as soon as 
6 possible. See id. at 104 ("Alternate Payee shall be paid Alternate Payee's benefits as 
7 soon as administratively feasible . . . and at the earliest date permitted under the terms of 
8 the Plan."). While the Plan terms forbid distribution prior to "retirement or termination" 
9 in sections 10.04 and 12.01(a), they contain a carveout for QDRO's. See ECF No. 216-5 
10 at 43, 49, 66–67. Section 18.04 reads in relevant part: 
11 A domestic relations order shall not fail to be deemed a qualified domestic 
 relations order merely because it permits distribution or requires segregation 
12 
 of all or part of a Participant's Account with respect to an alternate payee prior 
13 to the Participant's earliest retirement age (as defined in Code Section 414(p)) 
 under the Plan. A distribution to an alternate payee prior to the Participant's 
14 
 attainment of the earliest retirement age is available only if the order provides 
15 for distribution at that time and the alternate payee consents to a distribution 
 occurring prior to the Participant's attainment of earliest retirement age. 
16 
 Id. at 67. Thus, the Plan terms allow for early distribution as long as the QDRO provides 
17 
 for distribution at that time. That requirement is satisfied here, where the QDRO 
18 
 provided for distribution "as soon as administratively feasible . . . and at the earliest date 
19 
 permitted under the terms of the plan." Id. 
20 
 Plaintiff responds, first, that the Plan terms require the QDRO "expressly allow" 
21 
 for early distribution. ECF No. 228 at 15. He provides no legal citation for this 
22 
 requirement. As an initial matter, the word "expressly" is not found in the Plan terms. 
23 
 Even if it were, the QDRO expressly provides for distribution "as soon as feasible." 
24 
 Furthermore, in addition to failing to provide a citation for his argument, Plaintiff fails to 
25 
 explain what "expressly allow" would require. Plaintiff's motion does nothing more than 
26 
 regurgitate the language of the QDRO, highlighting and underlining portions of the Plan 
27 
1 terms. See ECF No. 228 at 15–16. Nowhere, does he articulate why the language of the 
2 QDRO is insufficient. Coupled with Plaintiff's failure to provide legal citation for his 
3 argument, Plaintiff's conclusory assertions leave the Court with no concrete argument to 
4 address. 
5 Next, Plaintiff argues that Dickerson v. Dickerson, 803 F. Supp. 127, 133 (E.D. 
6 Tenn. 1992), which was cited in this Court's order on Defendants' Motions to Dismiss, 
7 ECF No. 120, forbids the immediate distribution contemplated in the QDRO. But facts 
8 developed through discovery have rendered Dickerson distinguishable. In Dickerson, the 
9 court was asked whether a divorce decree constituted a valid QDRO where the decree 
10 required immediate distribution of the ex-husband's pension benefits—even though the 
11 ex-husband was not yet eligible for distribution, and the pension plan's terms did not 
12 provide for early distributions. Id. at 131. The court reasoned that the decree could not 
13 be a QDRO because it required the plan "to provide a benefit not otherwise permitted 
14 under the pension plan and to disburse pension funds to the alternate payee spouse prior 
15 to the participant James Dickerson reaching age 55 which is the earliest retirement age 
16 when he is eligible to begin receiving normal retirement benefits under the terms of the 
17 SERF plan." Id. at 134. In other words, the decree didn't qualify as a QDRO because it 
18 required immediate distribution in violation of the plan's terms. The court further 
19 concluded that 29 U.S.C. § 1056(d)(3)(E)—which provides certain exceptions by which 
20 "[a] domestic relations order shall not be treated as failing to meet the requirements of 
21 clause (i) of subparagraph (D)"—could not save the divorce decree, as these exceptions 
22 "presume[] that the participant has reached the age where he is eligible to receive 
23 retirement benefits under the terms of his pension plan." Id. at 133. 
24 Here, the Texas court order has already been determined to be a QDRO. ECF No. 
25 120. That alone distinguishes Dickerson, a case in which the court was determining 
26 whether the divorce decree constituted a QDRO. Indeed, Plaintiff's reliance on 
27 
1 Dickerson implicitly challenges the validity of the QDRO, an issue they have been 
2 explicitly barred from pursuing. Id. Furthermore, the plan in Dickerson did not allow for 
3 early distributions; the Plan, here, does. Accordingly, the instant QDRO does not require 
4 the Plan to "provide a benefit not otherwise permitted," and analysis of the exceptions 
5 laid out in 29 U.S.C. § 1056(d)(3)(E) is unnecessary. Thus, the Court rejects Plaintiff's 
6 reliance on Dickerson and GRANTS Defendants' Motion for Summary Judgment as to 
7 Plaintiff's first cause of action. 
8 II. Failure to Provide Written Procedures 
9 Plaintiff's second remaining cause of action (his fourth cause of action) attacks 
10 Ligand's failure to provide Plaintiff with a copy of the Plan's written procedures—
11 specifically, the written procedures for determining the qualified status of domestic 
12 relations orders. ECF No. 228 at 18. Again, Plaintiff must identify "downstream 
13 consequences" of that violation. ECF No. 120 at 32 (quoting TransUnion, 141 S. Ct. at 
14 2214). Again, Plaintiff has failed. 
15 ERISA requires that "[e]ach plan shall establish reasonable procedures to 
16 determine the qualified status of domestic relations orders." 29 U.S.C. § 1056(d)(G). 
17 These procedures must "be in writing" and, upon receipt of any domestic relations order, 
18 the plan must promptly provide such procedures to the "participant and each alternate 
19 payee" named in the order. Id. Defendants concede that their procedures were not 
20 written, and, accordingly, that written procedures were not provided to Plaintiff. ECF 
21 No. 216-5 at 213. Plaintiff alleges that this failure "depriv[ed] Plaintiff the knowledge of 
22 the appropriate avenue to protect his rights against an improperly obtained QDRO."4 
23 ECF No. 228 at 20. Plaintiff continues further, asserting that "Plaintiff had no idea that 
24 

25 
26 4 Plaintiff's assertion that the QDRO was "improperly obtained" again advances 
 arguments explicitly foreclosed by this Court's prior order. ECF No. 120 at 17–18. 
27 
1 the proper venue to challenge the validity of the 401(k) QDRO [wa]s in federal court." 
2 Id.; see also ECF No. 216-5 at 366 ("Exclusive jurisdiction . . . resides with the federal 
3 court . . . ."). 
4 Plaintiff's arguments rely upon several conclusions unsupported by the record, the 
5 law, and this Court's prior orders. To begin with, Plaintiff does not cite to any cases for 
6 his contention that the QDRO could only be properly challenged in federal court. That is 
7 because there are none. See generally Mack v. Kuckenmeister, 619 F.3d 1010, 1017 (9th 
8 Cir. 2010) (finding state court may "determine whether a DRO is a QDRO"); In re 
9 Marriage of Levingston, 12 Cal.App.4th 1303, 1304 (Cal. Ct. App. 1993) (same); 
10 Langston v. Wilson McShane Corp., 776 N.W.2d 684, 685 (Minn. 2009) (same); Dalton 
11 v. Dalton, 551 S.W.3d 126, 142 (Tex. 2018) ("[U]nder ERISA, the proposed order does 
12 not qualify as a QDRO."). More grievous, however, is that Plaintiff's argument runs 
13 afoul of this Court's previous holding on collateral estoppel. He argues, for instance, that 
14 he was harmed because he was denied "the opportunity to contest the validity of the 
15 401(k) QDRO in a trial court setting with discovery." ECF No. 228 at 20. This argument 
16 implies that the state court proceedings—where Plaintiff did contest the validity of the 
17 QDRO—were deficient, an argument this Court explicitly rejected when it found that the 
18 issues had been "fully and fairly litigated in Texas state court." ECF No. 120 at 20. 
19 Plaintiff next asserts, that he challenged the "QDRO through the Texas appellate court 
20 system, which denied his petition without any stated reason or analysis whatsoever," and 
21 that "[n]ow, Plaintiff has been prejudiced because he cannot challenge [the] validity of 
22 the 401(k) QDRO based on collateral estoppel." Id. at 21. What Plaintiff means by this 
23 is unclear; the language appears to operate as an extension of Plaintiff's previous 
24 discovery argument. To that end, the Court finds it unpersuasive for the reasons stated 
25 above. However, to the extent Plaintiff intends to raise an argument in the alternative, 
26 that he is injured merely because he litigated the issue in state court as opposed to federal 
27 
1 court, the Court rejects that argument for fresh reasons. Such an argument refuses to 
2 acknowledge a "fundamental precept of common-law adjudication, embodied in the . . . 
3 doctrine[] of collateral estoppel," Montana v. United States, 440 U.S. 147, 153 (1979), 
4 and the "full faith and credit" that federal courts must afford state court judgments, 
5 Kremer v. Chemical Construction Corp., 456 U.S. 461, 467 n.6 (1982). 
6 Additionally, even if this Court were to hold that Plaintiff's decision to challenge 
7 the QDRO in state court constitutes an "adverse effect," TransUnion, 141 S.Ct. at 2214, 
8 Plaintiff has failed to demonstrate that Ligand's procedural violation caused this injury. 
9 Plaintiff complains that Ligand failed to provide him with their procedures for 
10 determining the status of the QDRO. Ligand's Chief People Officer explained those 
11 procedures: 
12 Q: Okay. Can you describe the process for me? 
 A: We will receive a QDRO, and then we notify the individual that the QDRO 
13 
 has been received. Legal counsel will review it to confirm that it is a 
14 legitimate and accurate QDRO, and then we move forward with the 
 distribution based on what is listed in the QDRO. 
15 
 ECF No. 216-5 at 236. Plaintiff fails to explain how notice of these procedures would 
16 
 have altered his choice of forum. Ligand's practice of consulting with outside counsel 
17 
 prior to distribution would not affect Plaintiff's choice of forum. And Plaintiff does not 
18 
 identify any portion of the Chief People Officer's testimony or any other evidence in the 
19 
 record that would suggest that notice of these procedures would have included 
20 
 information about the differences between federal and state court or legal advice 
21 
 suggesting he should file suit in federal court. Instead, he directs this Court to the 
22 
 "Department of Labor's QDRO Booklet," ECF No. 228 at 19, which provides that "[i]t is 
23 
 the view of the Department of Labor that a plan's QDRO procedures should . . . includ[e] 
24 
 . . . [a] description of the process provided under the plan for obtaining a review of the 
25 
26 
27 
1 administrator's determination as to whether an order is a QDRO."5 He argues that if 
2 Ligand had complied with the Department of Labor's guidance, Ligand's procedures 
3 would have included information found in their Summary Plan Description ("SDP") 
4 which informs participants that they "may file suit in Federal court." ECF No. 228 at 20 
5 n.9. 
6 The Department of Labor's view is not binding on this Court, and the Court does 
7 not find its expanded interpretation persuasive. But assuming this Court were to adopt 
8 the Department's interpretation, Plaintiff's theory of injury requires two additional 
9 assumptions: that the Plan would have included information about obtaining an external 
10 judicial review of the determination—as opposed to an internal company appeal 
11 process—and that Plaintiff, upon reading such information, would have chosen to litigate 
12 in federal court.6 This theory of injury is far too speculative. See Carney v. Adams, 141 
13 S. Ct. 493, 498 (2020). Thus, for the reasons stated, the Court GRANTS Defendants' 
14 Motion for Summary Judgment as to Plaintiff's fourth cause of action. 
15 III. Retaliation 
16 Plaintiff's final cause of action alleges that Ligand retaliated against him for filing 
17 the instant lawsuit by reducing his bonus in 2018, limiting his merit increase in 2019, and 
18 terminating his employment in 2021. Defendants insist that Plaintiff waived this claim 
19 pursuant to the release contained in the parties' written 2019 and 2021 settlement 
20 agreements. Plaintiff maintains that the language of the release does not apply to (1) 
21 

22 
23 5 The Court takes sua sponte judicial notice of information publicly available on a 
 government webpage. Fed. R. Evid. 201(b)(2). 
24 
 6 In supplemental briefing, Plaintiff argues that Defendants failed to raise the issue of 
25 
 Article III standing in their initial briefing. Accordingly, he argues that those arguments 
26 must be disregarded. ECF No. 244 at 3. But the Court must sua sponte consider issues 
 of standing, and in any event, does not rely upon Defendants' arguments. 
27 
1 claims asserted in the FAC or (2) claims Plaintiff might make in association therewith. 
2 Retaliation, he contends, is the latter. 
3 It falls, then, to the Court to determine the scope of the negotiated release. The 
4 parties agree that interpretation of the 2019 and 2021 agreements is governed by 
5 California law. Under California law, a release is a contract, Solis v. Kirkwood Resort 
6 Co., 94 Cal. App. 4th 354, 360 (2001), and its interpretation is governed by the same 
7 principles applicable to any other contract, Matthews v. Atchison, T. & S.F. Ry. Co., 54 
8 Cal. App. 2d 549, 557 (1942), the goal being to achieve the parties' intent. Where the 
9 contract language is clear and unambiguous, its plain meaning governs. Cal. Civ. Code § 
10 1638. Where the contract language "appears . . . inconsistent," the Court considers the 
11 document as a whole, in context, each clause helping to interpret the other. Mike Rose's 
12 Auto Body, Inc. v. Applied Underwriters Captive Risk Assurance Co., No. 16-cv-01864-
13 EMC, 2016 WL 5407898 at *5 (N.D. Cal. Sep. 28, 2016). In this manner, the Court may 
14 resolve an initial ambiguity by giving effect to every part of a contract and avoiding an 
15 interpretation that leaves part of a contract as surplusage. Rice v. Downs, 248 Cal. App. 
16 4th 175, 186 (2016), as modified on denial of reh'g (June 23, 2016), as modified (June 28, 
17 2016). 
18 If the ambiguity remains unresolved, the Court "may consider extrinsic evidence to 
19 determine the parties' intent at the time the agreement was executed, including ‘earlier 
20 negotiations, later conduct, related agreements, and industrywide custom.'" Trustees of 
21 Operating Engineers Pension Trust v. Smith-Emery Co., Inc., 906 F. Supp. 2d 1043, 1054 
22 (C.D. Cal. 2012) (quoting Pierce County Hotel Employees & Restaurant Employees 
23 Health Trust v. Elks Lodge, 827 F.2d 1324, 1327 (9th Cir. 1987)). Where, after all of the 
24 above, ambiguity persists, "and contrary inferences as to intent are possible, there exists 
25 an issue of material fact for which summary judgment is ordinarily 
26 
27 
1 inappropriate." Arizona Laborers, Teamsters & Cement Masons Local 395 Health & 
2 Welfare Trust Fund v. Conquer Cartage Co., 753 F.2d 1512, 1518 (9th Cir. 1985). 
3 At the summary judgment stage, the Court must determine whether the contract 
4 may be interpreted as a matter of law. Before proceeding with its analysis, the Court 
5 furnishes further relevant facts. 
6 Following the dispute over the validity of the QDRO and Ligand's distribution of 
7 Plaintiff's retirement funds, Plaintiff worried that he might be "wrongfully terminated." 
8 ECF No. 216-5 at 384. In two notes, delivered to Ligand's president and general counsel 
9 prior to the filing of the FAC, Plaintiff detailed those worries. Id. at 369. The first note, 
10 sent February 11, 2019, described a conversation Plaintiff overheard where the Plan 
11 administrator asked the company's president to terminate Plaintiff. Id. at 375–376. 
12 Plaintiff wrote that such behavior "may be seen as retaliatory" and "that it obviously 
13 would be necessary to take precautions against wrongful termination." Id. In the second 
14 note, sent April 3, 2019, Plaintiff reported that "outside counsel, Jake Ryan, ha[d] hinted 
15 for Ligand to terminate [Plaintiff]." Id. at 378. 
16 Two months later, on June 19, 2019, Plaintiff filed the FAC, including nine claims 
17 related to Ligand's processing of the QDRO. ECF No. 42. The FAC did not plead a 
18 retaliation claim. Nor did it allege Plaintiff's salary-related reductions. But those 
19 concerns remained top-of-mind for Plaintiff, and so he retained John Vanderpool, an 
20 attorney that he believed "knew how to deal with retaliatory and wrongful termination 
21 matters." ECF No. 216-5 at 384–85. 
22 On July 29, 2019, following extensive negotiations, Plaintiff and Ligand entered 
23 into the 2019 Confidential Settlement Agreement ("2019 CSA"), to resolve the "dispute 
24 [that] ha[d] arisen between the Parties related to [Plaintiff]'s employment." ECF No. 
25 218-5 at 2. The 2019 CSA represents that "the Parties jointly participated in the drafting" 
26 and that the agreement was "freely and voluntarily entered into, and that each of the 
27 
1 Parties has had an opportunity to consult with counsel."7 ECF No. 218-5 at 9; ECF No. 
2 216-2 at ¶ 51. In accordance with the 2019 CSA, Plaintiff would continue working as a 
3 "business development executive" until December 31, 2022, at which point his 
4 employment would terminate. ECF No. 218-5 at 2–3. If Plaintiff were terminated 
5 without cause prior to that point, Ligand agreed to continue to pay Plaintiff his base 
6 salary, provide current-level benefits, and allow for full vesting of options through the 
7 time remaining. Id. at 7–8. In exchange, Plaintiff signed a "General Release," which 
8 "release[d] and discharge[d] Ligand and its Related Parties from any and all liabilities, 
9 claims, wages, agreements, obligations, demands and causes of action . . . arising out of 
10 or in any way connected with [Plaintiff]'s employment relationship with Ligand . . . ." 
11 Id. at 4. Section 8 of the 2019 CSA ("2019 Carveout") provided, however, that: 
12 Lundstrom is currently litigating Lundstrom v. Young et al. (Case No. 3:18-
 cv-02856) in the United States District Court for the Southern District of 
13 
 California (the "Ongoing Litigation"). Lundstrom has asserted claims against 
14 Ligand and the Ligand Pharmaceuticals Incorporated 401(k) Plan in the 
 Ongoing Litigation (the "Ongoing Litigation Claims"). The releases and 
15 
 covenants in Settlement Agreement shall not apply to the Ongoing Litigation 
16 Claims that Lundstrom has made or may make in association therewith. 
 Id. In 2021, following a separate licensing dispute, Ligand sought to accelerate 
17 
 Plaintiff's termination date, and the two parties entered into the 2021 Confidential 
18 
 Settlement Agreement ("2021 CSA"). The parties agreed that Plaintiff's employment 
19 
 would end on July 1, 2021, and that he would "never seek employment at Ligand or any 
20 
 of its affiliates" again. ECF No. 216-5 at 392. But until December 31, 2022, he would 
21 
 continue to be paid his monthly salary, his existing stock options would continue to vest, 
22 
23 

24 
 7 The 2021 Confidential Settlement agreement contains similar representations. 
25 
 Plaintiff's opposition asserts that the agreements were entirely prepared by Ligand, ECF 
26 No. 228 at 24, an assertion that Plaintiff's counsel repeated at oral argument. Upon 
 reviewing the document, however, Plaintiff's counsel conceded that he was mistaken. 
27 
1 and Ligand would continue to provide his health insurance. Id. In exchange, Plaintiff 
2 agreed to a second release, far more detailed than the general release contained in the 
3 2019 CSA. The 2021 CSA release provided that in exchange for the termination benefits 
4 set forth in the agreement, and except for those obligations created by or arising out of 
5 the "Ongoing Litigation": 
6 [Plaintiff] agrees to release and forever discharge Ligand . . . from any and all 
 claims . . . based on any events or circumstances arising or occurring on or 
7 
 prior to the date hereof, arising directly or indirectly out of, relating to, or in 
8 any other way involving in any manner whatsoever [Plaintiff]'s employment 
 by or service to Ligand and its Related Parties or the termination thereof, 
9 
 including any and all claims arising under federal, state, or local laws relating 
10 to employment, including without limitation claims of wrongful discharge . . 
 . and claims of any kind that may be brought in any court or administrative 
11 
 agency including, without limitation, claims under . . . the Employee 
12 Retirement Income Security Act . . . . 
 ECF No. 218-6 at 6–7. The 2021 CSA explicitly released claims of wrongful discharge 
13 
 and claims brought under ERISA arising from Plaintiff's termination. Section 8 of the 
14 
 2021 CSA ("2021 Carveout") largely mirrored the 2019 Carveout: 
15 
 Ongoing Litigation. Lundstrom is currently litigating Lundstrom v. Young et 
16 
 al. (No. 20-55002) in the United States District Court for the Southern District

 Lundstrom v. Young et al. (Case No. 37-2020-00039495-CU-NP-CTL) in the

 Superior Court of the State of California, County of San Diego (collectively

 Plan, as well as Carla Young, in the Ongoing Litigation (the "Ongoing

 Id. at 9. The question before the Court is whether Plaintiff's retaliation claim, which 
25 
 directly tracks the language of 2021 CSA release, was nevertheless preserved by the 2021 
26 
 Carveout as a claim "ma[d]e in association" with "Ongoing Litigation Claims." 
27 
1 Plaintiff urges the Court to adopt the plain language of the contract, reasoning that 
2 he was retaliated against for filing the "Ongoing Litigation Claims," and thus his 
3 retaliation claim is necessarily "made in association" with those claims. Defendants 
4 respond that as a matter of plain meaning, Plaintiff's retaliation claim is not "made in 
5 association" with an "Ongoing Litigation Claim" because "[i]t is an entirely new cause of 
6 action based on previously unpleaded operative facts." ECF No. 216-1 at 22. At the time 
7 the 2021 CSA was signed, "the operative pleading was the FAC, and all of [Plaintiff's] 
8 claims against Ligand in the FAC related to its administration of the two Texas domestic 
9 relations orders then at issue. There was no claim (or even allegation) that Ligand had 
10 retaliated against Lundstrom in violation of ERISA." Id. 
11 Viewed in isolation, the 2021 Carveout is arguably susceptible to both 
12 interpretations. Defendants correctly observe that the FAC contained no allegations or 
13 claims relating to wrongful discharge or retaliation. In all likelihood, Plaintiff could not 
14 have raised his retaliation claim in the FAC without alleging additional facts. In this 
15 sense, Plaintiff's retaliation claim is not one he "ha[d] made" or "m[ight] make in 
16 association therewith". But that does not decide the matter. Because the language of the 
17 2021 Carveout, alone, does not require such a strict and narrow interpretation. As 
18 Plaintiff emphasizes, "nothing in the ‘may make in association therewith' language . . . 
19 expressly limits Plaintiff from asserting new claims based on a different set of facts than 
20 those pled in the FAC." ECF No. 228 at 23. Read more charitably, the 2021 Carveout 
21 could preserve Plaintiff's retaliation claim: the claim was made in association with the 
22 "Ongoing Litigation Claims" because the claim would not exist but for the initiation of 
23 
24 
25 
26 
27 
1 the "Ongoing Litigation." Accordingly, an analysis constrained to the language of the 
2 2021 Carveout results in an ambiguity.8 
3 However, Plaintiff's interpretation sours in the framing of the entire document. 
4 The 2021 CSA release, though sprawling in its drafting, is specific in its subject matter. 
5 Plaintiff "releases . . . claims . . . relating to . . . [his] employment . . . or the termination 
6 thereof." ECF No. 218-6 at 6–7. Specifically enumerated are claims of "wrongful 
7 discharge" and claims related to Plaintiff's employment and termination brought under 
8 "the Employee Retirement Income Security Act." Id. Plaintiff's retaliation claim, 
9 essentially a claim for wrongful discharge brought under the Employee Retirement 
10 Income Security Act, tracks the release language perfectly. To adopt Plaintiff's 
11 interpretation of the 2021 Carveout, and to allow his retaliation claim to survive, would 
12 be to render these portions of the release language ineffectual. That is not what 
13 California law teaches. 
14 Only by limiting the reach of the 2021 Carveout may the Court give effect to every 
15 part of the contract. The Court construes the 2021 Carveout to provide safe harbor only 
16 to claims attacking Ligand's processing of the QDRO that could have been pleaded upon 
17 the facts asserted in the FAC. Under this construction, both the 2021 Carveout and the 
18 release language retain their effect. Plaintiff complains that such an interpretation robs 
19 the carveout clause of any meaning, but his past actions belie such an argument. 
20 Plaintiff's eleventh cause of action in his Second Amended Complaint takes full 
21 advantage of this Court's construction, alleging a new state law claim for negligence 
22 
23 
24 
25 
 8 Because the Court finds that the agreement is subject to some initial ambiguity, 
26 Plaintiff's argument in supplemental briefing—that Defendants should be estopped from 
 arguing that the contract is unambiguous—is moot. ECF No. 242 at 6. 
27 
1 arising out of Ligand's processing of the QDRO. SAC at 29–30. The 2021 Carveout 
2 preserved Plaintiff's right to pursue his negligence claim but not his retaliation.9 
3 To the extent any ambiguity persists, the extrinsic evidence confirms this Court's 
4 reading. The CSAs were negotiated following Plaintiff's notes regarding retaliation and 
5 wrongful discharge. The 2021 CSA differed from the 2019 CSA, explicitly including a 
6 release for claims relating to Plaintiff's termination, including claims of wrongful 
7 discharge and claims under the Employee Retirement Income Security Act. The parties' 
8 decision to include such additions cannot be ignored, much less rendered surplusage by 
9 Plaintiff's overly broad reading of the 2021 Carveout. Indeed, to adopt Plaintiff's 
10 reading in light of the background concerns about retaliation and wrongful discharge 
11 would create an absurd result: "his voluntary signing of the 2021 CSA, while represented 
12 by counsel, w[ould be] itself an act of retaliation that was not released by the 2021 CSA." 
13 See ECF No. 216-1 at 23; see also Cnty. of Humboldt v. McKee, 165 Cal. App. 4th 1476, 
14 1498, as modified on denial of reh'g (Sept. 10, 2008) (avoiding interpretations that create 
15 absurd result). Accordingly, this Court concludes that the 2021 CSA waives Plaintiff's 
16 right to pursue his retaliation claim. 
17 Plaintiff's final argument is that there exists a material dispute as to whether 
18 Plaintiff's waiver is enforceable. "Courts in this district have adopted the First Circuit's 
19 
20 
21 
 9 Plaintiff argues that under Singley v. Ill. & Midland R.R., this Court "should interpret 
22 the carveout to allow plaintiff to bring his ERISA retaliation claim. 24 F. Supp. 2d 900, 
 901 (C.D. Ill. 1998). But in Singley, the penultimate sentence of the release clause 
23 
 explicitly excepted ERISA claims. Id. ("This Agreement and/or Release does not restrict 
24 any rights or privileges the Employee is entitled to by virtue of the Company's 401(k) 
 plan and the Employee Retirement Income Security Act."). Relying on that "penultimate 
25 
 sentence," the court in Singley allowed plaintiff's retaliation claim. Id. at 903. Here, the 
26 CSA's release clause explicitly includes Plaintiff's ERISA claims. See ECF No. 216-2 at 
 ¶ 48. Singley is thus inapposite. 
27 
1 Morais test for determining whether a waiver of ERISA claims is knowing and voluntary. 
2 Under Morais, the court must examine the totality of the circumstances, including but not 
3 limited to the following six factors: ‘(1) plaintiff's education and business sophistication; 
4 (2) the respective roles of employer and employee in determining the provisions of the 
5 waiver; (3) the clarity of the agreement; (4) the time plaintiff had to study the agreement; 
6 (5) whether plaintiff had independent advice, such as that of counsel; and (6) the 
7 consideration for the waiver.'" Colaco v. Asic Advantage Simplified Employee Pension 
8 Plan, No. 5:13-cv-00972-PSG, at *7 (N.D. Cal. Sep. 24, 2015) (quoting Morais v. 
9 Central Beverage Corp. Union Employees' Supplemental Retirement Plan, 167 F.3d 709, 
10 713 (1st Cir. 1999)). Each factor favors Defendants. (1) Plaintiff is sophisticated; he has 
11 a "masters in sciences" and the "equivalent of an M.B.A." from Denmark. ECF No. 216-
12 5 at 352. (2) The construction of the CSA involved input from both parties' counsel. Id. 
13 at 396. (3) As discussed above, the Court finds the CSA to be clear. (4) Plaintiff had 
14 time to review several drafts of the agreement between May 21, 2021, and June 21, 2021, 
15 (5) with the "counsel of [his] choice." Id. (6) And in exchange for signing the CSA, 
16 Plaintiff continued to receive his salary for an additional year and four months following 
17 his termination. Plaintiff's motion only challenges the third factor, arguing that the CSA 
18 "lacks clarity and is ambiguous to the point that it creates a genuine issue of material fact 
19 as to whether Plaintiff's acceptance of the terms of the 2021 CSA constituted a voluntary, 
20 deliberate, and informed waiver of his right to pursue his ERISA retaliation claim against 
21 Ligand." This is an argument the Court has already rejected. Plaintiff does not challenge 
22 the voluntary nature of the waiver on any other grounds, and summarily concludes that 
23 there "exists a material disputed fact as to whether Plaintiff voluntarily entered into the 
24 2021 CSA and released his ERISA retaliation claim." Such a conclusory statement, 
25 without more, is not sufficient to stave off summary judgment. See California v. Trump, 
26 963 F.3d 926, 936 (9th Cir. 2020) (quoting Clapper v. Amensty Int'l USA, 568 U.S. 398, 
27 
1 (2013)) (‘At summary judgment, a plaintiff cannot rest on mere allegations, but 
2 || ‘must set forth by affidavit or other evidence specific facts."''). 
3 Because the Court concludes that the 2021 Carveout does not apply to Plaintiff's 
4 retaliation claim, and because Plaintiff has failed to direct the court to specific facts 
5 demonstrating a material dispute, the Court GRANTS Defendants' Motion for Summary 
6 || Judgment as to Plaintiff's twelfth cause of action. 
7 CONCLUSION 
8 The Court GRANTS Defendants' Motion for Summary Judgment on Plaintiff's 
9 || First, Fourth, and Twelfth causes of action. The Clerk shall enter judgment accordingly 
10 |/and close the case. Defendant's Motion to Exclude is DENIED as moot. ECF No. 215.
